title: Reducing food waste at home
subtitle: Storage, date labels, and meal planning: where the 70% avoidable waste goes
excerpt: UK households throw away 6.4 million tonnes of food each year, 70% of which was avoidable. WRAP data puts the average household saving from eliminating avoidable waste at per year. The largest single category is fresh fruit and vegetables (34% of avoidable waste), followed by drinks and bread.
sourceNotes: WRAP Household Food Waste in the UK 2021 report. WRAP Love Food Hate Waste campaign data. FSA UK date label guidance (use-by vs best-before).

content.text: UK households waste 6.4 million tonnes of food annually at a cost of around per household per year. The top wasted foods by weight are: fresh vegetables (23%), drinks (18%), fresh fruit (11%), bakery (8%), and dairy (7%). The pattern is consistent across income levels, food waste is not primarily a behaviour problem, it is a storage and purchasing problem.
Use-by: a safety date. Do not eat food after this date. Applies to ready meals, raw meat, soft cheese, and some dairy products.

Best-before: a quality date. Food is safe after this date but may have deteriorated in flavour, texture, or nutritional value. Applies to dried goods, tinned food, frozen food, hard cheese, eggs, and most packaged ambient products. There is no safety reason to throw food away on the best-before date.
Display until / sell by: for retailer stock management only. Not relevant to consumers.
Fridge at 3-5°C: above 8°C halves the shelf life of most perishables. Most UK home fridges run at 7°C or higher, check with a fridge thermometer.
FIFO (first in, first out): put new purchases at the back, older items at the front. Works for cupboards, fridge, and freezer.

Fruit and vegetables: most last longer in the fridge than on the counter. Exceptions: potatoes (not in fridge, starch converts to sugar below 6°C), tomatoes (not in fridge, lose flavour), bananas (not in fridge).
Bread: freeze the sliced loaf and take out slices as needed. Toast from frozen. A loaf lasts weeks in the freezer instead of days on the counter.
Buy to a list, not to offers: 3-for-2 on yogurt that goes off before you eat it is not a saving.
Portion sizing: WRAP data shows that preparing too much food, rather than not eating what is purchased, accounts for about 30% of household food waste.
End-of-week cook: one meal per week made from fridge remnants cuts the 'things that went off' category significantly.
